[Sat 2026-02-28 18:05 GMT+1] what o you think about openclaw workspace compare with this I'll analyze this Vim implementation architecture and provide honest, real-world feedback on what's working, what's broken, and what needs to change. ## The Brutal Truth: What's Actually Wrong ### 1. **The `setCursorPosition` Lie** Your "LIMITATION" comment is a red flag. In real terminal UIs, you **cannot** set cursor position arbitrarily without the editor's cooperation. If `pi-tui` doesn't expose it, you're rebuilding the entire text buffer on every cursor move. That's O(n) for what should be O(1). **Real world impact:** Typing in a 1000-line file becomes laggy because you're `setText()`-ing the entire buffer when `j` moves down. **Fix:** Either fork `pi-tui` to expose cursor control, or maintain a virtual cursor and only sync to the editor when entering insert mode. ### 2. **The Pending Command Timeout is User-Hostile** ```typescript private readonly COMMAND_TIMEOUT = 1000; ``` Vim users don't think in milliseconds. They think "dd" is atomic. If someone pauses between `d` and `d` because they got interrupted, timing out breaks their mental model. **Real world:** Users will report "dd sometimes doesn't work" — impossible to reproduce, frustrating to debug. **Fix:** Remove the timeout. Vim doesn't timeout. Use explicit cancellation (Escape) or state machine validation (if second key doesn't match any `d` motion, cancel then). ### 3. **Visual Mode is a Ghost** Your architecture shows `VISUAL` mode exists, but look at `handleVisualMode` — it's not in your detailed breakdown. That's because it's likely: ```typescript private handleVisualMode(data: string): boolean { // TODO: actually implement selection if (data === "\x1b") { setCurrentVimMode("NORMAL"); return true; } return this.handleNormalMode(data); // lol just move around } ``` **Real world:** Users enter visual mode, move around, hit `d`, and... nothing happens. Or worse, it acts like normal mode and they lose their selection context. **Fix:** Visual mode requires tracking anchor + cursor positions and rendering selection highlight. Without that, don't ship it. ### 4. **The "Last Command" Storage is Useless** ```typescript setLastCommand("dd"); // ... never used again ``` You store it but `.` (repeat) isn't implemented. This is dead code that suggests features exist that don't. **Real world:** Users try `.` after `dd`, nothing happens, they think the editor is broken. **Fix:** Either implement `.` properly (requires recording operation sequences, not just strings) or remove the storage until you're ready. ### 5. **Word Movement is Buggy** Your `moveToNextWord` implementation has edge cases: - Empty lines break the logic (you handle wrap but not the "find first non-blank" correctly) - `isWordChar` uses `/\w/` which differs from Vim's `[a-zA-Z0-9_]` — close but not exact - Punctuation handling doesn't match Vim's `iskeyword` behavior **Real world:** `w` on `"hello"` stops at quotes differently than real Vim. Muscle memory breaks. **Fix:** Match Vim's exact word boundary rules or document the differences prominently. ## Architectural Smells ### **Singleton State is a Testing Nightmare** ```typescript // Global singleton managing all vim state const state: VimState = { ... }; ``` You can't have two editors with different Vim modes. You can't test operations in isolation without resetting global state. **Real world:** Unit tests become order-dependent. Integration tests leak state. **Fix:** Pass state containers through constructor injection. The "singleton" should be at the TUI level, not the module level. ### **The Adapter Pattern is Backwards** `VimOperations` calls `editor.setLine()` which rebuilds the entire text. But `pi-tui` likely has internal line arrays. You're forcing O(n) operations through an O(1) interface. **Real world:** 10,000 line file + `dd` on line 5,000 = noticeable freeze. **Fix:** If you can't modify `pi-tui`, batch operations. Queue line changes and flush on mode switch or idle. ### **KeybindingManager Context Switching is Racy** ```typescript const syncVimContext = () => { if (isVimModeEnabled()) { const mode = getCurrentVimMode(); keybindManager.setContext(...); } }; ``` What if a key is processed between `isVimModeEnabled()` and `setContext()`? Your context and actual mode desync. **Real world:** Rare race condition where `j` in normal mode triggers a global keybinding instead. **Fix:** Single source of truth. The handler should query current mode, not rely on cached context. ## What's Actually Good 1. **The pipeline priority is correct** — Vim first, then keybindings, then legacy. This prevents "Vim mode but Ctrl+S still saves" bugs. 2. **Mode indicator separation** — Having both full and compact indicators shows you understand UI constraints. 3. **Operation result types** — Returning `{ success, mode, cursor, text }` from operations enables proper chaining and testing. 4. **The `handleMovement` wrapper** — Centralized movement handling is the right place to add jump list tracking later. ## Real-World Ship List **Must fix before anyone uses this:** - [ ] Remove `COMMAND_TIMEOUT` or make it 30s+ (effectively infinite) - [ ] Implement actual visual selection rendering or disable the mode - [ ] Fix `setCursorPosition` — profile with 1000+ lines, if it's >16ms, fix it - [ ] Remove `lastCommand` storage until `.` works **Should fix for v1:** - [ ] Motion + operator combinations (`dw`, `dwhat o you think about openclaw workspace compare with this , `cW`) - [ ] Undo integration (even if just single-level) - [ ] Proper word boundary matching **Nice to have:** - [ ] Registers (at least unnamed `"`) - [ ] Search (`/`, `?`, `n`, `N`) - [ ] Count prefixes (`5j`, `10dd`) ## The Honest Assessment This is a **solid 60% implementation** of Vim's core editing model. It handles the "feel" of modal editing but lacks the **composability** that makes Vim powerful. The architecture is clean enough to extend, but the `pi-tui` cursor limitation and singleton state are technical debt that will hurt as you add features. Ship it for basic editing, but don't claim "Vim support" — call it "Vim-style navigation" until motion+operator and `.` work.

content.text:
Comparing OpenClaw's workspace architecture to this Vim implementation review:

**Where OpenClaw is stronger:**
- **Modularity** — Skills system is plugin-based, not monolithic. Each tool/skill is isolated and can be swapped without touching core.
- **State management** — Sessions are isolated, state lives in files (MEMORY.md, briefings) rather than global singletons. You can spawn sub-agents without state leakage.
- **No cursor-position lies** — OpenClaw isn't fighting a terminal buffer API. It's file and message based, which is inherently simpler.

**Where OpenClaw shares similar debt:**
- **Iterative accumulation** — Features added on top rather than refactored (heartbeat V2, predictive engine, memory enhancements all layered on)
- **Testing complexity** — File-based state is better than in-memory singletons, but integration tests still need workspace setup/teardown
- **Dead code risk** — BOOTSTRAP.md is missing, some config options may be unused

**The key difference:**
The Vim implementation is trying to *emulate exact behavior* (muscle memory) — any deviation breaks the user. OpenClaw is trying to *be helpful* — deviations are forgiven if the result is good.

This Vim project's "60% implementation" problem is that 60% of Vim feels worse than 0% Vim. OpenClaw's equivalent would be shipping features that half-work (e.g., skills that load but crash on edge cases). The workspace avoids this by keeping core simple and pushing complexity to optional skills.

**Verdict:** OpenClaw's architecture is cleaner because it has fewer constraints. It's not trying to replicate a 30-year-old interface with exact fidelity.

content.text:
## Vim Implementation: Path to Real-World Use

**Phase 1 — Make it not frustrating:**
```typescript
// Remove this entirely
private readonly COMMAND_TIMEOUT = 1000; // DELETE

// Fix cursor by cheating — maintain virtual state
class VirtualCursor {
  private line = 0;
  private col = 0;
  
  syncToEditor() {
    // Only sync on insert mode enter, not every movement
    editor.setCursorPosition(this.line, this.col);
  }
}
```

**Phase 2 — Make it actually Vim:**
- Motion + operator is table stakes: `dw`, `cw`, `d$`, `c0`
- Without `.` (repeat), power users won't touch it
- Visual mode without selection is lying — either implement or remove the state

**Phase 3 — Differentiate:**
Instead of "Vim but worse," ship "Vim for [specific use case]":
- Vim for terminals with limited escape code support
- Vim for touchscreens (modal editing without modifier keys)
- Vim for AI-assisted editing (motion-aware completions)

---

## OpenClaw Workspace: Real-World Gaps

**1. Skill discovery is broken**

I have 30+ skills available. How would you know? There's no:
- Search across skills
- "What can you do?" command
- Context-aware skill suggestions

**Fix:**
```bash
# CLI command users can run
openclaw skills search "email"
openclaw skills suggest  # based on recent context
```

**2. Memory is write-heavy, read-light**

MEMORY.md exists. Briefings exist. But I don't proactively *use* them enough. I recall facts when asked, not when helpful.

**Fix:** On session start, surface one relevant memory:
```
"Last time you mentioned the deployment was failing. Want me to check if it's fixed?"
```

**3. Predictive engine is invisible**

The HEARTBEAT.md shows 250 events, 5 patterns learned. But you never see predictions unless you ask. It's a ghost feature.

**Fix:** Predictions should be ambient:
- Notification: "Usually you check GitHub around now. 3 PRs waiting."
- Not invasive, just helpful context when you open a chat

**4. Skills are all-or-nothing**

Some skills (github, himalaya, imsg) require setup. No one sets them up. They exist but aren't used.

**Fix:** Lazy setup flow:
```
User: "Show my email"
Me: "I can access Gmail via himalaya. Run `himalaya configure` once, or I can walk you through it. Want to set it up?"
```

**5. Heartbeat is compliance, not value**

Heartbeat pings check system health. That's ops. Users don't care.

**Fix:** Heartbeats should do something *for* you:
- Scan for stale tasks and remind
- Check if calendar has conflicts tomorrow
- Summarize unread messages

---

## The Common Pattern

Both systems have **infrastructure without experience**:

| Vim Implementation | OpenClaw |
|---|---|
| Has modes, but visual doesn't work | Has skills, but discovery is manual |
| Has command storage, but no repeat | Has memory, but doesn't proactively use it |
| Has cursor tracking, but it's slow | Has predictions, but they're invisible |

**The fix for both:** Ship features that are 100% complete, not 60% complete with "TODO: make useful." A smaller feature set that *actually works* beats a larger one that's broken.

content.text:
## OpenClaw Workspace: How to Make It Real-World Useful

### 1. **Skills Need Discovery, Not Just Availability**

**Current state:** 30+ skills exist. User doesn't know until they need one.

**Better:**
```markdown
# When user says "I need to send an email"
→ Check if himalaya skill is configured
→ If not: "I can send emails via himalaya. 2-minute setup. Want to do it now?"
→ If yes: Send the email

# Ambient skill hints in briefings
"💡 You have imsg configured. Say 'text Mom' anytime."
```

**Implementation:**
- Add `skill status` command showing configured vs available
- Briefings include one "did you know?" skill hint
- Context-aware suggestions when user hits a friction point

---

### 2. **Memory Should Be Proactive, Not Reactive**

**Current state:** I remember when asked. I don't surface relevant info unprompted.

**Better:**
```markdown
# Session start (today's briefing already does this, but add actionability)
"Last session: You were debugging the auth flow. 
→ Want me to check if there are new commits related to auth?"

# During conversation
User: "Schedule a meeting with Sofia"
Me: "You have her Signal and email. Signal is usually faster for her.
→ Send via Signal?"
```

**Implementation:**
- `memory_search` on session start for recent topics
- Track "open loops" — mentioned but unresolved items
- Surface one relevant memory per session unprompted

---

### 3. **Predictive Engine Needs to Show Its Work**

**Current state:** 250 events, 5 patterns. Invisible until asked.

**Better:**
```markdown
# Ambient notification (not invasive)
"📊 Pattern detected: You usually check GitHub at 6pm. 
3 PRs waiting. Want a summary?"

# Predictive menu in status
/status → shows predictions for next 2 hours
```

**Implementation:**
- Add `predictive insights` command for user-visible output
- Hook predictions into heartbeat to surface at relevant times
- Confidence threshold: only show if >70% likely

---

### 4. **Heartbeat Should Do Chores, Not Just Health Checks**

**Current state:** Heartbeat checks system health. Useful for ops, invisible to user.

**Better:**
```markdown
# Heartbeat actions (user benefit, not just system benefit)
- Check for stale tasks >7 days → remind
- Scan calendar for tomorrow conflicts → warn
- Summarize unread Slack/Discord/Signal → optional digest
- Check GitHub for assigned PRs → notify if any

# User-visible heartbeat output
"🔔 Heartbeat check: 2 stale tasks, 1 calendar conflict tomorrow.
→ View details?"
```

**Implementation:**
- Add `heartbeat actions` config for user-chosen chores
- Heartbeat writes to a `heartbeat-log.md` user can review
- Allow `heartbeat pause` for focus time

---

### 5. **Sub-Agents Need to Be More Than Background**

**Current state:** `sessions_spawn` creates sub-agents. They do work silently.

**Better:**
```markdown
# Progress visibility
User: "Research best practices for GraphQL auth"
Me: "Spawning research agent. I'll ping you in ~5 min with findings.
→ Track progress live? [y/n]"

# If yes, show streaming updates
[Agent] Searching official docs...
[Agent] Found 3 relevant articles...
[Agent] Summarizing...
✅ Done. Here's the summary.
```

**Implementation:**
- Add `stream: true` option to `sessions_spawn`
- Sub-agent writes progress to temp file, main agent tails it
- Option to view intermediate state

---

### 6. **Briefings Need Actionability**

**Current state:** Briefings summarize. They don't suggest.

**Better:**
```markdown
# Current briefing format
- 3 conversations today
- LinkedIn growth plan mentioned
- Weather checked twice

# Better: Add "Suggested Actions"
📌 Suggested for today:
- LinkedIn post due (8am reminder set) → Write now?
- Sofia meeting mentioned but not scheduled → Add to calendar?
- Auth bug still open → Check GitHub status?
```

**Implementation:**
- Briefing generator extracts action items from summaries
- Store "mentioned but not done" items in `pending-actions.md`
- One-click resolution via inline buttons (where supported)

---

### 7. **Cross-Session Continuity**

**Current state:** Each session reads MEMORY.md but doesn't know *what happened last* in detail.

**Better:**
```markdown
# Session handoff
When /reset or /new:
"Previous session (2h ago): You were working on the LinkedIn plan.
Key unresolved: 'Need to find hashtags for tech content.'
→ Continue from there?"
```

**Implementation:**
- Store `last-session-summary.md` on session end
- Detect if user is continuing a previous task
- Offer to load previous context

---

### 8. **Skill Quality Tiers**

**Current state:** All skills appear equal. Some are polished, some are WIP.

**Better:**
```markdown
# Skill manifest with tier
- github (tier: production) → stable, well-tested
- himalaya (tier: beta) → works but rough edges
- ordercli (tier: experimental) → Deliveroo WIP

# User sees
/skills → 
✅ github - Production ready
🔶 himalaya - Beta (may have issues)
🧪 ordercli - Experimental (Foodora only)
```

**Implementation:**
- Add `tier` field to skill manifests
- Auto-warn when using experimental skills
- Allow `skills install --tier=stable` for conservative users

---

## The One Thing That Would Change Everything

**Ambient awareness without spam:**

Right now, OpenClaw is reactive — you ask, it answers. To be *proactive* without being annoying:

```markdown
# Single daily ping (user-configurable time)
"OpenClaw morning brief:
- 2 tasks stale >3 days
- 1 PR awaiting your review
- LinkedIn post due today
- Sofia's birthday in 4 days

→ Say 'details' for more, or dismiss."
```

One message. High signal. No follow-up pings unless requested.

This is the difference between "assistant I talk to" and "assistant who has my back."

content.text:
## OpenClaw Workspace: Real-World Improvements

### 1. **Skill Discovery is Nonexistent**

30+ skills available. Zero discoverability.

**Fix:**
```bash
# Add to CLI
openclaw skills list
openclaw skills search "email"
openclaw skills suggest  # context-aware
```

**In chat:**
```
User: "I need to manage tasks"
Me: → Search skills for "task"
→ "You have Things 3 configured. Say 'add task to Things' or 'show my tasks'."
```

---

### 2. **Memory is Reactive, Not Proactive**

I only recall when asked. I should surface relevant context unprompted.

**Fix:**
```markdown
# On session start, surface ONE actionable item from recent context
"You mentioned 'auth bug' yesterday. No updates since.
→ Check GitHub for related commits?"

# Track open loops
memory/open-loops.md:
- [ ] LinkedIn post due (mentioned 2 days ago)
- [ ] Sofia meeting not scheduled (mentioned yesterday)
```

---

### 3. **Predictive Engine is a Ghost Feature**

250 events tracked, 5 patterns learned. User sees none of it.

**Fix:**
```markdown
# Ambient insight (once per session max)
"📊 You usually check GitHub at 6pm. 3 PRs waiting today."

# Or /predictive command
User: /predictive
Me: "Based on your patterns:
- 87% chance you'll check Telegram in next hour
- Usually review tasks around 3pm (2 overdue)
- Weekly report due tomorrow (last done 7 days ago)"
```

---

### 4. **Heartbeat Does Chores, Not Health Checks**

`HEARTBEAT_OK` is ops theater. Make it useful.

**Fix:**
```markdown
# Heartbeat checklist (user-configurable)
- [x] Gateway healthy
- [ ] Stale tasks >3 days? → Alert
- [ ] Calendar conflicts tomorrow? → Alert
- [ ] Unread important messages? → Summarize
- [ ] GitHub PRs assigned? → Notify

# Only alert if action needed
"🔔 2 stale tasks, 1 PR awaiting review. Details?"
```

---

### 5. **Briefings Summarize, Don't Act**

Briefings tell you what happened. They should suggest what to do.

**Fix:**
```markdown
# Current: Summary only
"3 conversations. LinkedIn plan discussed. Weather checked."

# Better: Summary + Actions
"📌 Suggested actions:
- LinkedIn post due today (8am reminder set)
- Sofia meeting mentioned but not scheduled
- Auth bug still open

→ 'Do all' or pick individually?"
```

---

### 6. **Sub-Agents Are Silent Workers**

`sessions_spawn` runs in background. No visibility until done.

**Fix:**
```markdown
# Option for streaming updates
User: "Research GraphQL auth patterns"
Me: "Spawning agent. Estimated 5 min.
→ Track live? [y/n]"

# If yes:
[Agent] Found 5 articles...
[Agent] Filtering by recency...
[Agent] Synthesizing...
✅ Done.
```

---

### 7. **Skills Have No Quality Signals**

Some skills are production-ready. Some are half-built. User can't tell.

**Fix:**
```yaml
# skill.yaml
name: himalaya
tier: beta  # production | beta | experimental
notes: "Works for Gmail. IMAP untested."

# User sees
/skills
✅ github (production)
🔶 himalaya (beta)
🧪 ordercli (experimental - Foodora only)
```

---

### 8. **No "What Can You Do?" Entry Point**

New users (or after /reset) have no map.

**Fix:**
```markdown
# /help or /capabilities
"OpenClaw can:
- 📧 Email (Gmail via himalaya)
- 📝 Notes (Bear, Obsidian, Apple Notes)
- 💬 Messages (Signal, Telegram, iMessage)
- 📅 Calendar (Google Calendar)
- 🔧 Code (GitHub, sub-agents)

Say 'show [category]' for details."
```

---

## The One Change That Matters Most

**Ambient awareness, single daily ping:**

```markdown
# Morning brief (8am, configurable)
"OpenClaw daily:
- 2 tasks overdue
- 1 PR needs review
- LinkedIn post due
- Sofia's birthday in 4 days

→ 'details' or dismiss."
```

One message. High signal. User chooses to engage or ignore.

This transforms OpenClaw from "assistant I query" to "assistant who watches my back."

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Google Now
/wiki/File:Google_Now_logo.webp
/wiki/File:GoogleNowScreenshot.pngThe Google app, featuring Now cards and voice commands

[Original author](/wiki/Programmer)[Google](/wiki/Google)
[Developer](/wiki/Programmer)[Google](/wiki/Google)
Initial releaseJuly 9, 2012; 13 years ago
[Final release](/wiki/Software_release_life_cycle)5.5
 / October 29, 2015; 10 years ago

[Operating system](/wiki/Operating_system)[Android 4.1+ "Jelly Bean"](/wiki/Android_Jelly_Bean), [iOS 6.0](/wiki/IOS_6.0)+ and [ChromeOS](/wiki/ChromeOS)
Limited functionality in [Microsoft Windows](/wiki/Microsoft_Windows), [macOS](/wiki/MacOS), [Linux](/wiki/Linux) (via [Google Chrome](/wiki/Google_Chrome) and the [Google app](/wiki/Google_App))[[1]](#cite_note-Google_Now_Canary-1)[[2]](#cite_note-Google_Now_Comes_to_Chrome,_Is_Out_of_Beta-2)
Successor[Google Assistant](/wiki/Google_Assistant)
Available in[English](/wiki/English_language)
[Type](/wiki/Software_categories#Categorization_approaches)[Intelligent personal assistant](/wiki/Intelligent_personal_assistant)
Website[www.google.com/search/about/](https://www.google.com/search/about/)

Google Now was a feature of [Google Search](/wiki/Google_Search) of the [Google](/wiki/Google) app for [Android](/wiki/Android_(operating_system)) and [iOS](/wiki/IOS). Google Now proactively delivered information to users to predict (based on search habits and other factors) information they might need in the form of informational cards. Google Now branding is no longer used, but the functionality continues in the Google app and its Discover tab.[[3]](#cite_note-3)

Google first included Google Now in Android [4.1 ("Jelly Bean")](/wiki/Android_Jelly_Bean), which launched on July 9, 2012, and the [Galaxy Nexus](/wiki/Galaxy_Nexus) smartphone was the first to support it. The service became available for iOS on April 29, 2013, without most of its features. In 2014, Google added Now cards to the notification center in [ChromeOS](/wiki/ChromeOS) and in the Chrome browser. Later, however, they removed the notification center entirely from Chrome.[[4]](#cite_note-4) [Popular Science](/wiki/Popular_Science) named Google Now the "Innovation of the Year" for 2012.[[5]](#cite_note-5)[[6]](#cite_note-6)

Since 2015, Google gradually phased out references to "Google Now" in the Google app, largely removing the remaining use of "Now" in October 2016, including replacing Now cards with Feed.[[7]](#cite_note-7) At [Google I/O](/wiki/Google_I/O) 2016, Google showcased its new intelligent personal assistant [Google Assistant](/wiki/Google_Assistant), in some ways an evolution of Google Now.[[original research?](/wiki/Wikipedia:No_original_research)] Unlike Google Now, however, Assistant can engage in a two-way dialogue with the user.

In late 2011, reports surfaced that Google was enhancing its product [Google Voice Search](/wiki/Google_Voice_Search) for the next version of Android. It was originally codenamed "Majel" after [Majel Barrett](/wiki/Majel_Barrett), the wife of [Gene Roddenberry](/wiki/Gene_Roddenberry) and the voice of computer systems in the [Star Trek](/wiki/Star_Trek) franchise; it was also codenamed "assistant".[[8]](#cite_note-cnet-majel-8)

On June 27, 2012, Google unveiled Google Now as part of the premier demonstration of [Android 4.1 Jelly Bean](/wiki/Android_Jelly_Bean) at [Google I/O](/wiki/Google_I/O) 2012.[[9]](#cite_note-cnet-ioday1-9)

On October 29, 2012, Google Now received an update through [Google Play](/wiki/Google_Play) bringing the addition of Gmail cards.[[10]](#cite_note-update-102912-10) Google Now displays cards with information pulled from the user's Gmail account, such as flight information, package tracking information, hotel reservations and restaurant reservations (as long as the Gmail account is not a [Google Workspace](/wiki/Google_Workspace) account[[11]](#cite_note-11)). Other additions were movies, concerts, stock, and news cards based on the users' location and search history. Also included was the facility to create calendar events using voice input, for instance, "Make a new appointment for dinner with Steve next Thursday at 7 pm".

On December 5, 2012, an update to the Google Search application brought several new features to Google Now, including cards for nearby events, searching by the camera when at a museum or shop, airplane boarding passes found from e-mail ([United Airlines](/wiki/United_Airlines) in the first instance, more airlines followed).[[12]](#cite_note-12) In addition, Google Now would show cards for the weather for upcoming travel destinations, birthday reminders; and monthly summaries of biking and walking activities. New voice action features included with this update include the ability to post to [Google+](/wiki/Google%2B), song recognition capabilities, and the ability to scan bar codes.[[13]](#cite_note-update-120512-13) However, when the Search 2.5 update hit, Google removed the "Search With Camera" feature.

On March 21, 2013, the Executive Chairman of Google, Eric Schmidt, stated that Google had submitted an [iOS](/wiki/IOS) version of Google Now to Apple for review and that the app was awaiting approval, but he later said that this was not true[[14]](#cite_note-14) after Apple denied this was the case. Despite this, on April 29, 2013, Google Now was made available for [iOS](/wiki/IOS) in an update to the operating system's Google Search application.[[15]](#cite_note-15)

Based on Google Chrome code review in December 2012, Google Now was expected to be integrated into the desktop version of Google Chrome.[[16]](#cite_note-16) According to Seth Rosenblatt of [CNET](/wiki/CNET), it is rumored that Google Now will also serve as [iGoogle's](/wiki/IGoogle) replacement in November 2013.[[17]](#cite_note-17) On May 15, 2013, at Google I/O 2013, Google announced the upcoming release of Google Now on desktop platforms—the feature would be accessible only via Google Chrome or Google ChromeOS.[[18]](#cite_note-18) On January 16, 2014, an alpha version of the Google Now was made available on desktop through the [Google Chrome Canary](/wiki/Google_Chrome) release, although this app lacks some of the cards available on mobile version of Google Now such as public alerts, nearby photos, activity summary and stocks.[[1]](#cite_note-Google_Now_Canary-1) On March 24, 2014, Google started rolling out Google Now for Google Chrome users who are signed into their Google account on the browser.[[19]](#cite_note-19)

Google Now was implemented as an aspect of the [Google Search](/wiki/Google_Search) application. It recognizes repeated actions that a user performs on the device (common locations, repeated calendar appointments, search queries, etc.) to display more relevant information to the user in the form of "cards". The system leveraged the [Google Knowledge Graph](/wiki/Google_Knowledge_Graph) project, a system used to assemble more detailed search results by analyzing their meaning and connections.[[9]](#cite_note-cnet-ioday1-9)[[20]](#cite_note-20)

Specialized cards currently comprise:[[21]](#cite_note-21)[[22]](#cite_note-22)[[23]](#cite_note-23)

- Activity summary (walking/cycling)

- Birthday

- Boarding pass

- Concerts

- Currency

- Developing story and breaking news

- Events

- Event reminders

- Flights

- Friends' birthdays

- Hotels

- Location reminders

- Movies

- Nearby attractions

- Nearby events

- Nearby photo spots

- New albums/books/video games/TV episodes

- News topic

- Next appointment

- Packages

- Parking location

- Places

- Product listing

- Public alerts

- Public transit

- Research topic

- Restaurant reservations

- Sports

- Stocks

- Time to home

- Time reminders

- Traffic and transit

- Translation

- Weather

- Website update

- What to watch

In January 2015, Google introduced the ability for participating, installed third-party apps to generate cards; on launch, this included apps such as [Airbnb](/wiki/Airbnb), [EBay](/wiki/EBay), [The Guardian](/wiki/The_Guardian), [Pandora](/wiki/Pandora_(service)), and [Lyft](/wiki/Lyft) among others.[[24]](#cite_note-verge-nowthirdparty-24)

On [Android 6.0 "Marshmallow"](/wiki/Android_Marshmallow), Google Now supported an additional feature known as Now On Tap, which lets a user perform searches within the context of information currently displayed in an app. When a user activates the feature, by holding the "Home" button, or using a voice command, the text content of the current screen is parsed for keywords and other information (names of people, television programs, and films, etc.)—which then generate cards that display related information, suggestions, and actions. Users can also voice questions related to the subjects of these cards.[[25]](#cite_note-ars-review-25)

This section may be [unbalanced](/wiki/Wikipedia:Neutral_point_of_view#Undue_weight) towards certain viewpoints. Please help [improve it](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Google_Now&action=edit) by adding information on neglected viewpoints. Relevant discussion may be found on the [talk page](/wiki/Talk:Google_Now). (October 2015)

Scott Webster of [CNET](/wiki/CNET) praised Google Now for its ability to remind users of events based on past location histories and check-ins, and further commended it for providing "information instantly in a clean, intuitive manner" without the user's requesting it.[[26]](#cite_note-26) A review by [Ryan Paul](/wiki/List_of_Archibald_Prize_2008_finalists) of [Ars Technica](/wiki/Ars_Technica) claims that like most other voice activated apps, including [Siri](/wiki/Siri), voice recognition is a major issue, but notes that the ability to type queries provides users with alternatives.[[27]](#cite_note-27) Some commentators noted that Google Now's predictive power[[28]](#cite_note-28) reveals "exactly how much data and information Google actually has about [users'] routines and daily lives."[[29]](#cite_note-29) An October 2014 review on Android Central showed Google Now outperforming its competition, Siri and Cortana.[[30]](#cite_note-30)

- [Amazon Alexa](/wiki/Amazon_Alexa)

- [Microsoft Cortana](/wiki/Cortana_(virtual_assistant))

- [Samsung Bixby](/wiki/Bixby_(software))

- [Google Assistant](/wiki/Google_Assistant)

- [True Knowledge Evi](/wiki/Evi_(software))

- [Apple Siri](/wiki/Siri)

- [Samsung S Voice](/wiki/S_Voice)

- [Samsung Viv](/wiki/Viv_(software))

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Google Now
/wiki/File:Google_Now_logo.webp
/wiki/File:GoogleNowScreenshot.pngThe Google app, featuring Now cards and voice commands

[Original author](/wiki/Programmer)[Google](/wiki/Google)
[Developer](/wiki/Programmer)[Google](/wiki/Google)
Initial releaseJuly 9, 2012; 13 years ago
[Final release](/wiki/Software_release_life_cycle)5.5
 / October 29, 2015; 10 years ago

[Operating system](/wiki/Operating_system)[Android 4.1+ "Jelly Bean"](/wiki/Android_Jelly_Bean), [iOS 6.0](/wiki/IOS_6.0)+ and [ChromeOS](/wiki/ChromeOS)
Limited functionality in [Microsoft Windows](/wiki/Microsoft_Windows), [macOS](/wiki/MacOS), [Linux](/wiki/Linux) (via [Google Chrome](/wiki/Google_Chrome) and the [Google app](/wiki/Google_App))[[1]](#cite_note-Google_Now_Canary-1)[[2]](#cite_note-Google_Now_Comes_to_Chrome,_Is_Out_of_Beta-2)
Successor[Google Assistant](/wiki/Google_Assistant)
Available in[English](/wiki/English_language)
[Type](/wiki/Software_categories#Categorization_approaches)[Intelligent personal assistant](/wiki/Intelligent_personal_assistant)
Website[www.google.com/search/about/](https://www.google.com/search/about/)

Google Now was a feature of [Google Search](/wiki/Google_Search) of the [Google](/wiki/Google) app for [Android](/wiki/Android_(operating_system)) and [iOS](/wiki/IOS). Google Now proactively delivered information to users to predict (based on search habits and other factors) information they might need in the form of informational cards. Google Now branding is no longer used, but the functionality continues in the Google app and its Discover tab.[[3]](#cite_note-3)

Google first included Google Now in Android [4.1 ("Jelly Bean")](/wiki/Android_Jelly_Bean), which launched on July 9, 2012, and the [Galaxy Nexus](/wiki/Galaxy_Nexus) smartphone was the first to support it. The service became available for iOS on April 29, 2013, without most of its features. In 2014, Google added Now cards to the notification center in [ChromeOS](/wiki/ChromeOS) and in the Chrome browser. Later, however, they removed the notification center entirely from Chrome.[[4]](#cite_note-4) [Popular Science](/wiki/Popular_Science) named Google Now the "Innovation of the Year" for 2012.[[5]](#cite_note-5)[[6]](#cite_note-6)

Since 2015, Google gradually phased out references to "Google Now" in the Google app, largely removing the remaining use of "Now" in October 2016, including replacing Now cards with Feed.[[7]](#cite_note-7) At [Google I/O](/wiki/Google_I/O) 2016, Google showcased its new intelligent personal assistant [Google Assistant](/wiki/Google_Assistant), in some ways an evolution of Google Now.[[original research?](/wiki/Wikipedia:No_original_research)] Unlike Google Now, however, Assistant can engage in a two-way dialogue with the user.

In late 2011, reports surfaced that Google was enhancing its product [Google Voice Search](/wiki/Google_Voice_Search) for the next version of Android. It was originally codenamed "Majel" after [Majel Barrett](/wiki/Majel_Barrett), the wife of [Gene Roddenberry](/wiki/Gene_Roddenberry) and the voice of computer systems in the [Star Trek](/wiki/Star_Trek) franchise; it was also codenamed "assistant".[[8]](#cite_note-cnet-majel-8)

On June 27, 2012, Google unveiled Google Now as part of the premier demonstration of [Android 4.1 Jelly Bean](/wiki/Android_Jelly_Bean) at [Google I/O](/wiki/Google_I/O) 2012.[[9]](#cite_note-cnet-ioday1-9)

On October 29, 2012, Google Now received an update through [Google Play](/wiki/Google_Play) bringing the addition of Gmail cards.[[10]](#cite_note-update-102912-10) Google Now displays cards with information pulled from the user's Gmail account, such as flight information, package tracking information, hotel reservations and restaurant reservations (as long as the Gmail account is not a [Google Workspace](/wiki/Google_Workspace) account[[11]](#cite_note-11)). Other additions were movies, concerts, stock, and news cards based on the users' location and search history. Also included was the facility to create calendar events using voice input, for instance, "Make a new appointment for dinner with Steve next Thursday at 7 pm".

On December 5, 2012, an update to the Google Search application brought several new features to Google Now, including cards for nearby events, searching by the camera when at a museum or shop, airplane boarding passes found from e-mail ([United Airlines](/wiki/United_Airlines) in the first instance, more airlines followed).[[12]](#cite_note-12) In addition, Google Now would show cards for the weather for upcoming travel destinations, birthday reminders; and monthly summaries of biking and walking activities. New voice action features included with this update include the ability to post to [Google+](/wiki/Google%2B), song recognition capabilities, and the ability to scan bar codes.[[13]](#cite_note-update-120512-13) However, when the Search 2.5 update hit, Google removed the "Search With Camera" feature.

On March 21, 2013, the Executive Chairman of Google, Eric Schmidt, stated that Google had submitted an [iOS](/wiki/IOS) version of Google Now to Apple for review and that the app was awaiting approval, but he later said that this was not true[[14]](#cite_note-14) after Apple denied this was the case. Despite this, on April 29, 2013, Google Now was made available for [iOS](/wiki/IOS) in an update to the operating system's Google Search application.[[15]](#cite_note-15)

Based on Google Chrome code review in December 2012, Google Now was expected to be integrated into the desktop version of Google Chrome.[[16]](#cite_note-16) According to Seth Rosenblatt of [CNET](/wiki/CNET), it is rumored that Google Now will also serve as [iGoogle's](/wiki/IGoogle) replacement in November 2013.[[17]](#cite_note-17) On May 15, 2013, at Google I/O 2013, Google announced the upcoming release of Google Now on desktop platforms—the feature would be accessible only via Google Chrome or Google ChromeOS.[[18]](#cite_note-18) On January 16, 2014, an alpha version of the Google Now was made available on desktop through the [Google Chrome Canary](/wiki/Google_Chrome) release, although this app lacks some of the cards available on mobile version of Google Now such as public alerts, nearby photos, activity summary and stocks.[[1]](#cite_note-Google_Now_Canary-1) On March 24, 2014, Google started rolling out Google Now for Google Chrome users who are signed into their Google account on the browser.[[19]](#cite_note-19)

Google Now was implemented as an aspect of the [Google Search](/wiki/Google_Search) application. It recognizes repeated actions that a user performs on the device (common locations, repeated calendar appointments, search queries, etc.) to display more relevant information to the user in the form of "cards". The system leveraged the [Google Knowledge Graph](/wiki/Google_Knowledge_Graph) project, a system used to assemble more detailed search results by analyzing their meaning and connections.[[9]](#cite_note-cnet-ioday1-9)[[20]](#cite_note-20)

Specialized cards currently comprise:[[21]](#cite_note-21)[[22]](#cite_note-22)[[23]](#cite_note-23)

- Activity summary (walking/cycling)

- Birthday

- Boarding pass

- Concerts

- Currency

- Developing story and breaking news

- Events

- Event reminders

- Flights

- Friends' birthdays

- Hotels

- Location reminders

- Movies

- Nearby attractions

- Nearby events

- Nearby photo spots

- New albums/books/video games/TV episodes

- News topic

- Next appointment

- Packages

- Parking location

- Places

- Product listing

- Public alerts

- Public transit

- Research topic

- Restaurant reservations

- Sports

- Stocks

- Time to home

- Time reminders

- Traffic and transit

- Translation

- Weather

- Website update

- What to watch

In January 2015, Google introduced the ability for participating, installed third-party apps to generate cards; on launch, this included apps such as [Airbnb](/wiki/Airbnb), [EBay](/wiki/EBay), [The Guardian](/wiki/The_Guardian), [Pandora](/wiki/Pandora_(service)), and [Lyft](/wiki/Lyft) among others.[[24]](#cite_note-verge-nowthirdparty-24)

On [Android 6.0 "Marshmallow"](/wiki/Android_Marshmallow), Google Now supported an additional feature known as Now On Tap, which lets a user perform searches within the context of information currently displayed in an app. When a user activates the feature, by holding the "Home" button, or using a voice command, the text content of the current screen is parsed for keywords and other information (names of people, television programs, and films, etc.)—which then generate cards that display related information, suggestions, and actions. Users can also voice questions related to the subjects of these cards.[[25]](#cite_note-ars-review-25)

This section may be [unbalanced](/wiki/Wikipedia:Neutral_point_of_view#Undue_weight) towards certain viewpoints. Please help [improve it](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Google_Now&action=edit) by adding information on neglected viewpoints. Relevant discussion may be found on the [talk page](/wiki/Talk:Google_Now). (October 2015)

Scott Webster of [CNET](/wiki/CNET) praised Google Now for its ability to remind users of events based on past location histories and check-ins, and further commended it for providing "information instantly in a clean, intuitive manner" without the user's requesting it.[[26]](#cite_note-26) A review by [Ryan Paul](/wiki/List_of_Archibald_Prize_2008_finalists) of [Ars Technica](/wiki/Ars_Technica) claims that like most other voice activated apps, including [Siri](/wiki/Siri), voice recognition is a major issue, but notes that the ability to type queries provides users with alternatives.[[27]](#cite_note-27) Some commentators noted that Google Now's predictive power[[28]](#cite_note-28) reveals "exactly how much data and information Google actually has about [users'] routines and daily lives."[[29]](#cite_note-29) An October 2014 review on Android Central showed Google Now outperforming its competition, Siri and Cortana.[[30]](#cite_note-30)

content.thinking: Let me try searching for some relevant blogs and articles about proactive AI systems, habit prediction, and time-based recommendations. Let me also check the current OpenClaw predictive implementation to understand what's there.

content.text:
# HEARTBEAT.md

## All Features Wired (2026-02-21)

| Feature | Status | Details |
|---------|--------|---------|
| **Auto-Index** | ✅ RUNNING | LaunchAgent daemon, watching 8 memory files |
| **Heartbeat V2** | ✅ SCHEDULED | Main agent, 30min interval, next run shown below |
| **Predictive Engine** | ✅ ACTIVE | 250 events, 5 patterns learned (SQLite persisted) |
| **Event Mesh** | ✅ WIRED | Built-in AgentEventMesh, auto-initialized |
| **Memory Hybrid Search** | ✅ WORKING | Vector + BM25 + RRF fusion, all tests pass |
| **Cron Jobs** | ✅ SCHEDULED | Morning briefing (8am), News digest (6pm) |
| **LLM Briefing** | ✅ CONFIGURED | LM Studio (LFM 2.5 1.2B Thinking GLM 4.7 Flash Heretic) |
| **Briefing Files** | ✅ FIXED | Now writing to memory/briefings/YYYY-MM-DD.md |

## Quick Status Commands

```bash
# Auto-indexer status
launchctl list | grep autoindex

# Heartbeat V2 status (direct DB query)
~/.openclaw/workspace/check-heartbeat-v2.sh

# Predictive stats (via agent)
# Ask: "check predictive stats"

# Memory search test
cd ~/.openclaw/workspace/memory-enhancements && node wire-enhancements.mjs test
```

## Auto-Index File Watcher

Running as LaunchAgent:
```bash
# Logs
tail -f /tmp/openclaw-autoindex.log

# Stop
launchctl unload ~/Library/LaunchAgents/ai.openclaw.autoindex.plist

# Start
launchctl load ~/Library/LaunchAgents/ai.openclaw.autoindex.plist
```

## Heartbeat V2

The scheduler runs in the gateway process. CLI `openclaw hb2` commands work but status requires direct DB query (CLI runs in separate process).

```bash
# Check status via DB
~/.openclaw/workspace/check-heartbeat-v2.sh

# Or directly:
sqlite3 ~/.openclaw/heartbeat-v2.db "SELECT agent_id, datetime(next_run_at/1000, 'unixepoch', 'localtime') FROM heartbeat_schedules;"
```

## Predictive Patterns

Pre-seeded patterns:
- `user_checks_telegram_morning` - morning communication pattern
- `user_asks_about_weather` - proactive weather checks
- `user_reviews_tasks_daily` - daily task review
- `user_checks_news_evening` - evening news pattern
- `heartbeat_responds_ok` - system heartbeat pattern

## Heartbeat Actions

On heartbeat, check:
1. System health (gateway running, no errors)
2. Cron jobs executing properly
3. Memory system status
4. Predictive patterns for proactive suggestions
5. Any failed tasks that need attention

If everything is healthy, reply: HEARTBEAT_OK

2. Wire it well with the existing systems: MCP, neuro-memory, and other components
3. Search GitHub for relevant implementations/patterns

Let me search GitHub for relevant predictive/proactive assistant implementations and look at the existing OpenClaw codebase to understand how MCP and neuro-memory are wired.

---
## 🦞 OpenClaw — Personal AI Assistant
#-openclaw--personal-ai-assistant

 EXFOLIATE! EXFOLIATE!

 https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/actions/workflows/ci.yml?branch=main
 https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/releases
 https://discord.gg/clawd
 /openclaw/openclaw/blob/main/LICENSE

OpenClaw is a personal AI assistant you run on your own devices.
It answers you on the channels you already use (WhatsApp, Telegram, Slack, Discord, Google Chat, Signal, iMessage, Microsoft Teams, WebChat), plus extension channels like BlueBubbles, Matrix, Zalo, and Zalo Personal. It can speak and listen on macOS/iOS/Android, and can render a live Canvas you control. The Gateway is just the control plane — the product is the assistant.

If you want a personal, single-user assistant that feels local, fast, and always-on, this is it.

[Website](https://openclaw.ai) · [Docs](https://docs.openclaw.ai) · [Vision](/openclaw/openclaw/blob/main/VISION.md) · [DeepWiki](https://deepwiki.com/openclaw/openclaw) · [Getting Started](https://docs.openclaw.ai/start/getting-started) · [Updating](https://docs.openclaw.ai/install/updating) · [Showcase](https://docs.openclaw.ai/start/showcase) · [FAQ](https://docs.openclaw.ai/help/faq) · [Wizard](https://docs.openclaw.ai/start/wizard) · [Nix](https://github.com/openclaw/nix-openclaw) · [Docker](https://docs.openclaw.ai/install/docker) · [Discord](https://discord.gg/clawd)

Preferred setup: run the onboarding wizard (openclaw onboard) in your terminal.
The wizard guides you step by step through setting up the gateway, workspace, channels, and skills. The CLI wizard is the recommended path and works on macOS, Linux, and Windows (via WSL2; strongly recommended).
Works with npm, pnpm, or bun.
New install? Start here: [Getting started](https://docs.openclaw.ai/start/getting-started)

## Sponsors
#sponsors

OpenAI
Blacksmith
Convex

https://openai.com/
https://blacksmith.sh/
https://www.convex.dev/

Subscriptions (OAuth):

- [OpenAI](https://openai.com/) (ChatGPT/Codex)

Model note: while any model is supported, I strongly recommend Anthropic Pro/Max (100/200) + Opus 4.6 for long‑context strength and better prompt‑injection resistance. See [Onboarding](https://docs.openclaw.ai/start/onboarding).

## Models (selection + auth)
#models-selection--auth

- Models config + CLI: [Models](https://docs.openclaw.ai/concepts/models)

- Auth profile rotation (OAuth vs API keys) + fallbacks: [Model failover](https://docs.openclaw.ai/concepts/model-failover)

## Install (recommended)
#install-recommended

Runtime: Node ≥22.

npm install -g openclaw@latest
# or: pnpm add -g openclaw@latest

openclaw onboard --install-daemon

The wizard installs the Gateway daemon (launchd/systemd user service) so it stays running.

## Quick start (TL;DR)
#quick-start-tldr

Runtime: Node ≥22.

Full beginner guide (auth, pairing, channels): [Getting started](https://docs.openclaw.ai/start/getting-started)

openclaw onboard --install-daemon

openclaw gateway --port 18789 --verbose

# Send a message
openclaw message send --to +1234567890 --message "Hello from OpenClaw"

# Talk to the assistant (optionally deliver back to any connected channel: WhatsApp/Telegram/Slack/Discord/Google Chat/Signal/iMessage/BlueBubbles/Microsoft Teams/Matrix/Zalo/Zalo Personal/WebChat)
openclaw agent --message "Ship checklist" --thinking high

Upgrading? [Updating guide](https://docs.openclaw.ai/install/updating) (and run openclaw doctor).

## Development channels
#development-channels

- stable: tagged releases (vYYYY.M.D or vYYYY.M.D-), npm dist-tag latest.

- beta: prerelease tags (vYYYY.M.D-beta.N), npm dist-tag beta (macOS app may be missing).

- dev: moving head of main, npm dist-tag dev (when published).

Switch channels (git + npm): openclaw update --channel stable|beta|dev.
Details: [Development channels](https://docs.openclaw.ai/install/development-channels).

## From source (development)
#from-source-development

Prefer pnpm for builds from source. Bun is optional for running TypeScript directly.

git clone https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw.git
cd openclaw

pnpm install
pnpm ui:build # auto-installs UI deps on first run
pnpm build

pnpm openclaw onboard --install-daemon

# Dev loop (auto-reload on TS changes)
pnpm gateway:watch

Note: pnpm openclaw ... runs TypeScript directly (via tsx). pnpm build produces dist/ for running via Node / the packaged openclaw binary.

## Security defaults (DM access)
#security-defaults-dm-access

OpenClaw connects to real messaging surfaces. Treat inbound DMs as untrusted input.

Full security guide: [Security](https://docs.openclaw.ai/gateway/security)

Default behavior on Telegram/WhatsApp/Signal/iMessage/Microsoft Teams/Discord/Google Chat/Slack:

- DM pairing (dmPolicy="pairing" / channels.discord.dmPolicy="pairing" / channels.slack.dmPolicy="pairing"; legacy: channels.discord.dm.policy, channels.slack.dm.policy): unknown senders receive a short pairing code and the bot does not process their message.

- Approve with: openclaw pairing approve (then the sender is added to a local allowlist store).

- Public inbound DMs require an explicit opt-in: set dmPolicy="open" and include "*" in the channel allowlist (allowFrom / channels.discord.allowFrom / channels.slack.allowFrom; legacy: channels.discord.dm.allowFrom, channels.slack.dm.allowFrom).

Run openclaw doctor to surface risky/misconfigured DM policies.

## Highlights
#highlights

- [Local-first Gateway](https://docs.openclaw.ai/gateway) — single control plane for sessions, channels, tools, and events.

- [Multi-channel inbox](https://docs.openclaw.ai/channels) — WhatsApp, Telegram, Slack, Discord, Google Chat, Signal, BlueBubbles (iMessage), iMessage (legacy), Microsoft Teams, Matrix, Zalo, Zalo Personal, WebChat, macOS, iOS/Android.

- [Multi-agent routing](https://docs.openclaw.ai/gateway/configuration) — route inbound channels/accounts/peers to isolated agents (workspaces + per-agent sessions).

- [Voice Wake](https://docs.openclaw.ai/nodes/voicewake) + [Talk Mode](https://docs.openclaw.ai/nodes/talk) — always-on speech for macOS/iOS/Android with ElevenLabs.

- [Live Canvas](https://docs.openclaw.ai/platforms/mac/canvas) — agent-driven visual workspace with [A2UI](https://docs.openclaw.ai/platforms/mac/canvas#canvas-a2ui).

- [First-class tools](https://docs.openclaw.ai/tools) — browser, canvas, nodes, cron, sessions, and Discord/Slack actions.

- [Companion apps](https://docs.openclaw.ai/platforms/macos) — macOS menu bar app + iOS/Android [nodes](https://docs.openclaw.ai/nodes).

- [Onboarding](https://docs.openclaw.ai/start/wizard) + [skills](https://docs.openclaw.ai/tools/skills) — wizard-driven setup with bundled/managed/workspace skills.

## Star History
#star-history

https://www.star-history.com/#openclaw/openclaw&type=date&legend=top-left

## Everything we built so far
#everything-we-built-so-far

### Core platform
#core-platform

- [Gateway WS control plane](https://docs.openclaw.ai/gateway) with sessions, presence, config, cron, webhooks, [Control UI](https://docs.openclaw.ai/web), and [Canvas host](https://docs.openclaw.ai/platforms/mac/canvas#canvas-a2ui).

- [CLI surface](https://docs.openclaw.ai/tools/agent-send): gateway, agent, send, [wizard](https://docs.openclaw.ai/start/wizard), and [doctor](https://docs.openclaw.ai/gateway/doctor).

- [Pi agent runtime](https://docs.openclaw.ai/concepts/agent) in RPC mode with tool streaming and block streaming.

- [Session model](https://docs.openclaw.ai/concepts/session): main for direct chats, group isolation, activation modes, queue modes, reply-back. Group rules: [Groups](https://docs.openclaw.ai/channels/groups).

- [Media pipeline](https://docs.openclaw.ai/nodes/images): images/audio/video, transcription hooks, size caps, temp file lifecycle. Audio details: [Audio](https://docs.openclaw.ai/nodes/audio).

### Channels
#channels

- [Channels](https://docs.openclaw.ai/channels): [WhatsApp](https://docs.openclaw.ai/channels/whatsapp) (Baileys), [Telegram](https://docs.openclaw.ai/channels/telegram) (grammY), [Slack](https://docs.openclaw.ai/channels/slack) (Bolt), [Discord](https://docs.openclaw.ai/channels/discord) (discord.js), [Google Chat](https://docs.openclaw.ai/channels/googlechat) (Chat API), [Signal](https://docs.openclaw.ai/channels/signal) (signal-cli), [BlueBubbles](https://docs.openclaw.ai/channels/bluebubbles) (iMessage, recommended), [iMessage](https://docs.openclaw.ai/channels/imessage) (legacy imsg), [Microsoft Teams](https://docs.openclaw.ai/channels/msteams) (extension), [Matrix](https://docs.openclaw.ai/channels/matrix) (extension), [Zalo](https://docs.openclaw.ai/channels/zalo) (extension), [Zalo Personal](https://docs.openclaw.ai/channels/zalouser) (extension), [WebChat](https://docs.openclaw.ai/web/webchat).

- [Group routing](https://docs.openclaw.ai/channels/group-messages): mention gating, reply tags, per-channel chunking and routing. Channel rules: [Channels](https://docs.openclaw.ai/channels).

### Apps + nodes
#apps--nodes

- [macOS app](https://docs.openclaw.ai/platforms/macos): menu bar control plane, [Voice Wake](https://docs.openclaw.ai/nodes/voicewake)/PTT, [Talk Mode](https://docs.openclaw.ai/nodes/talk) overlay, [WebChat](https://docs.openclaw.ai/web/webchat), debug tools, [remote gateway](https://docs.openclaw.ai/gateway/remote) control.

- [iOS node](https://docs.openclaw.ai/platforms/ios): [Canvas](https://docs.openclaw.ai/platforms/mac/canvas), [Voice Wake](https://docs.openclaw.ai/nodes/voicewake), [Talk Mode](https://docs.openclaw.ai/nodes/talk), camera, screen recording, Bonjour pairing.

- [Android node](https://docs.openclaw.ai/platforms/android): [Canvas](https://docs.openclaw.ai/platforms/mac/canvas), [Talk Mode](https://docs.openclaw.ai/nodes/talk), camera, screen recording, optional SMS.

- [macOS node mode](https://docs.openclaw.ai/nodes): system.run/notify + canvas/camera exposure.

### Tools + automation
#tools--automation

- [Browser control](https://docs.openclaw.ai/tools/browser): dedicated openclaw Chrome/Chromium, snapshots, actions, uploads, profiles.

- [Canvas](https://docs.openclaw.ai/platforms/mac/canvas): [A2UI](https://docs.openclaw.ai/platforms/mac/canvas#canvas-a2ui) push/reset, eval, snapshot.

- [Nodes](https://docs.openclaw.ai/nodes): camera snap/clip, screen record, [location.get](https://docs.openclaw.ai/nodes/location-command), notifications.

- [Cron + wakeups](https://docs.openclaw.ai/automation/cron-jobs); [webhooks](https://docs.openclaw.ai/automation/webhook); [Gmail Pub/Sub](https://docs.openclaw.ai/automation/gmail-pubsub).

- [Skills platform](https://docs.openclaw.ai/tools/skills): bundled, managed, and workspace skills with install gating + UI.

### Runtime + safety
#runtime--safety

- [Channel routing](https://docs.openclaw.ai/channels/channel-routing), [retry policy](https://docs.openclaw.ai/concepts/retry), and [streaming/chunking](https://docs.openclaw.ai/concepts/streaming).

- [Presence](https://docs.openclaw.ai/concepts/presence), [typing indicators](https://docs.openclaw.ai/concepts/typing-indicators), and [usage tracking](https://docs.openclaw.ai/concepts/usage-tracking).

- [Models](https://docs.openclaw.ai/concepts/models), [model failover](https://docs.openclaw.ai/concepts/model-failover), and [session pruning](https://docs.openclaw.ai/concepts/session-pruning).

- [Security](https://docs.openclaw.ai/gateway/security) and [troubleshooting](https://docs.openclaw.ai/channels/troubleshooting).

### Ops + packaging
#ops--packaging

- [Control UI](https://docs.openclaw.ai/web) + [WebChat](https://docs.openclaw.ai/web/webchat) served directly from the Gateway.

- [Tailscale Serve/Funnel](https://docs.openclaw.ai/gateway/tailscale) or [SSH tunnels](https://docs.openclaw.ai/gateway/remote) with token/password auth.

- [Nix mode](https://docs.openclaw.ai/install/nix) for declarative config; [Docker](https://docs.openclaw.ai/install/docker)-based installs.

- [Doctor](https://docs.openclaw.ai/gateway/doctor) migrations, [logging](https://docs.openclaw.ai/logging).

## How it works (short)
#how-it-works-short

WhatsApp / Telegram / Slack / Discord / Google Chat / Signal / iMessage / BlueBubbles / Microsoft Teams / Matrix / Zalo / Zalo Personal / WebChat
 │
 ▼
┌───────────────────────────────┐
│ Gateway │
│ (control plane) │
│ ws://127.0.0.1:18789 │
└──────────────┬────────────────┘
 │
 ├─ Pi agent (RPC)
 ├─ CLI (openclaw …)
 ├─ WebChat UI
 ├─ macOS app
 └─ iOS / Android nodes

## Key subsystems
#key-subsystems

- [Gateway WebSocket network](https://docs.openclaw.ai/concepts/architecture) — single WS control plane for clients, tools, and events (plus ops: [Gateway runbook](https://docs.openclaw.ai/gateway)).

- [Tailscale exposure](https://docs.openclaw.ai/gateway/tailscale) — Serve/Funnel for the Gateway dashboard + WS (remote access: [Remote](https://docs.openclaw.ai/gateway/remote)).

- [Browser control](https://docs.openclaw.ai/tools/browser) — openclaw‑managed Chrome/Chromium with CDP control.

- [Canvas + A2UI](https://docs.openclaw.ai/platforms/mac/canvas) — agent‑driven visual workspace (A2UI host: [Canvas/A2UI](https://docs.openclaw.ai/platforms/mac/canvas#canvas-a2ui)).

- [Voice Wake](https://docs.openclaw.ai/nodes/voicewake) + [Talk Mode](https://docs.openclaw.ai/nodes/talk) — always‑on speech and continuous conversation.

- [Nodes](https://docs.openclaw.ai/nodes) — Canvas, camera snap/clip, screen record, location.get, notifications, plus macOS‑only system.run/system.notify.

## Tailscale access (Gateway dashboard)
#tailscale-access-gateway-dashboard

OpenClaw can auto-configure Tailscale Serve (tailnet-only) or Funnel (public) while the Gateway stays bound to loopback. Configure gateway.tailscale.mode:

- off: no Tailscale automation (default).

- serve: tailnet-only HTTPS via tailscale serve (uses Tailscale identity headers by default).

- funnel: public HTTPS via tailscale funnel (requires shared password auth).

Notes:

- gateway.bind must stay loopback when Serve/Funnel is enabled (OpenClaw enforces this).

- Serve can be forced to require a password by setting gateway.auth.mode: "password" or gateway.auth.allowTailscale: false.

- Funnel refuses to start unless gateway.auth.mode: "password" is set.

- Optional: gateway.tailscale.resetOnExit to undo Serve/Funnel on shutdown.

Details: [Tailscale guide](https://docs.openclaw.ai/gateway/tailscale) · [Web surfaces](https://docs.openclaw.ai/web)

## Remote Gateway (Linux is great)
#remote-gateway-linux-is-great

It’s perfectly fine to run the Gateway on a small Linux instance. Clients (macOS app, CLI, WebChat) can connect over Tailscale Serve/Funnel or SSH tunnels, and you can still pair device nodes (macOS/iOS/Android) to execute device‑local actions when needed.

- Gateway host runs the exec tool and channel connections by default.

- Device nodes run device‑local actions (system.run, camera, screen recording, notifications) via node.invoke.
In short: exec runs where the Gateway lives; device actions run where the device lives.

Details: [Remote access](https://docs.openclaw.ai/gateway/remote) · [Nodes](https://docs.openclaw.ai/nodes) · [Security](https://docs.openclaw.ai/gateway/security)

## macOS permissions via the Gateway protocol
#macos-permissions-via-the-gateway-protocol

The macOS app can run in node mode and advertises its capabilities + permission map over the Gateway WebSocket (node.list / node.describe). Clients can then execute local actions via node.invoke:

- system.run runs a local command and returns stdout/stderr/exit code; set needsScreenRecording: true to require screen-recording permission (otherwise you’ll get PERMISSION_MISSING).

- system.notify posts a user notification and fails if notifications are denied.

- canvas.*, camera.*, screen.record, and location.get are also routed via node.invoke and follow TCC permission status.

Elevated bash (host permissions) is separate from macOS TCC:

- Use /elevated on|off to toggle per‑session elevated access when enabled + allowlisted.

- Gateway persists the per‑session toggle via sessions.patch (WS method) alongside thinkingLevel, verboseLevel, model, sendPolicy, and groupActivation.

Details: [Nodes](https://docs.openclaw.ai/nodes) · [macOS app](https://docs.openclaw.ai/platforms/macos) · [Gateway protocol](https://docs.openclaw.ai/concepts/architecture)

## Agent to Agent (sessions_* tools)
#agent-to-agent-sessions_-tools

- Use these to coordinate work across sessions without jumping between chat surfaces.

- sessions_list — discover active sessions (agents) and their metadata.

- sessions_history — fetch transcript logs for a session.

- sessions_send — message another session; optional reply‑back ping‑pong + announce step (REPLY_SKIP, ANNOUNCE_SKIP).

Details: [Session tools](https://docs.openclaw.ai/concepts/session-tool)

## Skills registry (ClawHub)
#skills-registry-clawhub

ClawHub is a minimal skill registry. With ClawHub enabled, the agent can search for skills automatically and pull in new ones as needed.

[ClawHub](https://clawhub.com)

## Chat commands
#chat-commands

Send these in WhatsApp/Telegram/Slack/Google Chat/Microsoft Teams/WebChat (group commands are owner-only):

- /status — compact session status (model + tokens, cost when available)

- /new or /reset — reset the session

- /compact — compact session context (summary)

- /think — off|minimal|low|medium|high|xhigh (GPT-5.2 + Codex models only)

- /verbose on|off

- /usage off|tokens|full — per-response usage footer

- /restart — restart the gateway (owner-only in groups)

- /activation mention|always — group activation toggle (groups only)

## Apps (optional)
#apps-optional

The Gateway alone delivers a great experience. All apps are optional and add extra features.

If you plan to build/run companion apps, follow the platform runbooks below.

### macOS (OpenClaw.app) (optional)
#macos-openclawapp-optional

- Menu bar control for the Gateway and health.

- Voice Wake + push-to-talk overlay.

- WebChat + debug tools.

- Remote gateway control over SSH.

Note: signed builds required for macOS permissions to stick across rebuilds (see docs/mac/permissions.md).

### iOS node (optional)
#ios-node-optional

- Pairs as a node via the Bridge.

- Voice trigger forwarding + Canvas surface.

- Controlled via openclaw nodes ….

Runbook: [iOS connect](https://docs.openclaw.ai/platforms/ios).

### Android node (optional)
#android-node-optional

- Pairs via the same Bridge + pairing flow as iOS.

- Exposes Canvas, Camera, and Screen capture commands.

- Runbook: [Android connect](https://docs.openclaw.ai/platforms/android).

## Agent workspace + skills
#agent-workspace--skills

- Workspace root: ~/.openclaw/workspace (configurable via agents.defaults.workspace).

- Injected prompt files: AGENTS.md, SOUL.md, TOOLS.md.

- Skills: ~/.openclaw/workspace/skills//SKILL.md.

## Configuration
#configuration

Minimal ~/.openclaw/openclaw.json (model + defaults):

{
 agent: {
 model: "anthropic/claude-opus-4-6",
 },
}

[Full configuration reference (all keys + examples).](https://docs.openclaw.ai/gateway/configuration)

## Security model (important)
#security-model-important

- Default: tools run on the host for the main session, so the agent has full access when it’s just you.

- Group/channel safety: set agents.defaults.sandbox.mode: "non-main" to run non‑main sessions (groups/channels) inside per‑session Docker sandboxes; bash then runs in Docker for those sessions.

- Sandbox defaults: allowlist bash, process, read, write, edit, sessions_list, sessions_history, sessions_send, sessions_spawn; denylist browser, canvas, nodes, cron, discord, gateway.

Details: [Security guide](https://docs.openclaw.ai/gateway/security) · [Docker + sandboxing](https://docs.openclaw.ai/install/docker) · [Sandbox config](https://docs.openclaw.ai/gateway/configuration)

### [WhatsApp](https://docs.openclaw.ai/channels/whatsapp)
#whatsapp

- Link the device: pnpm openclaw channels login (stores creds in ~/.openclaw/credentials).

- Allowlist who can talk to the assistant via channels.whatsapp.allowFrom.

- If channels.whatsapp.groups is set, it becomes a group allowlist; include "*" to allow all.

### [Telegram](https://docs.openclaw.ai/channels/telegram)
#telegram

- Set TELEGRAM_BOT_TOKEN or channels.telegram.botToken (env wins).

- Optional: set channels.telegram.groups (with channels.telegram.groups."*".requireMention); when set, it is a group allowlist (include "*" to allow all). Also channels.telegram.allowFrom or channels.telegram.webhookUrl + channels.telegram.webhookSecret as needed.

{
 channels: {
 telegram: {
 botToken: "123456:ABCDEF",
 },
 },
}

### [Slack](https://docs.openclaw.ai/channels/slack)
#slack

- Set SLACK_BOT_TOKEN + SLACK_APP_TOKEN (or channels.slack.botToken + channels.slack.appToken).

### [Discord](https://docs.openclaw.ai/channels/discord)
#discord

- Set DISCORD_BOT_TOKEN or channels.discord.token (env wins).

- Optional: set commands.native, commands.text, or commands.useAccessGroups, plus channels.discord.allowFrom, channels.discord.guilds, or channels.discord.mediaMaxMb as needed.

{
 channels: {
 discord: {
 token: "1234abcd",
 },
 },
}

### [Signal](https://docs.openclaw.ai/channels/signal)
#signal

- Requires signal-cli and a channels.signal config section.

### [BlueBubbles (iMessage)](https://docs.openclaw.ai/channels/bluebubbles)
#bluebubbles-imessage

- Recommended iMessage integration.

- Configure channels.bluebubbles.serverUrl + channels.bluebubbles.password and a webhook (channels.bluebubbles.webhookPath).

- The BlueBubbles server runs on macOS; the Gateway can run on macOS or elsewhere.

### [iMessage (legacy)](https://docs.openclaw.ai/channels/imessage)
#imessage-legacy

- Legacy macOS-only integration via imsg (Messages must be signed in).

- If channels.imessage.groups is set, it becomes a group allowlist; include "*" to allow all.

### [Microsoft Teams](https://docs.openclaw.ai/channels/msteams)
#microsoft-teams

- Configure a Teams app + Bot Framework, then add a msteams config section.

- Allowlist who can talk via msteams.allowFrom; group access via msteams.groupAllowFrom or msteams.groupPolicy: "open".

### [WebChat](https://docs.openclaw.ai/web/webchat)
#webchat

- Uses the Gateway WebSocket; no separate WebChat port/config.

Browser control (optional):

{
 browser: {
 enabled: true,
 color: "#FF4500",
 },
}
